summary: DHH responds to Robert Scoble's question about whether anyone is actually using dynamic alternatives to ASP.NET by showcasing testimonials from former .NET developers who switched to Ruby on Rails. He curates quotes from developers who describe dramatically higher productivity with Rails compared to ASP.NET, including one who says his '.NET career was over' within hours of discovering Rails. DHH also points to growing community metrics with over 1,100 inbound links to Rails sites. He closes by acknowledging that developers stuck in .NET for political reasons can look at MonoRail, a .NET project inspired by Rails.
key_points: Former .NET developers report dramatically higher productivity after switching to Rails, even with years of .NET experience
Rails eliminates the need for heavyweight IDEs and complex boilerplate layers (DALs, stored procedures, server controls) that .NET requires
The Rails community was growing rapidly with over 1,100 inbound links tracked on Technorati
Even developers forced to stay on .NET were bringing Rails ideas into that ecosystem via projects like MonoRail
money_quotes: I have been FAR more productive with RoR after just a few months of learning Ruby and a few weeks of using RoR than I am with .NET even though I've been coding on the MS platform for 10 years.
Two hours later my .NET career was over. It literally happened that afternoon.
I no longer had any interest in NHibernate or even seeing Visual Studio ever again.
Going back to ASP.Net development makes me feel dirty.
What? I have to update the stored procedure, the model, the DAL, the form, and create a new server control just to add a property?
I don't need an ultra-powerful IDE like VS to develop RoR apps. I do just fine with VIM, thank you.
key_insight: DHH uses the voices of converted .NET developers themselves to make the case that Rails' simplicity and productivity were pulling programmers away from Microsoft's enterprise stack.
